Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time on weekends and maybe a couple evenings during the week, but with limited resources and having to share devices at home sometimes, it wasn't always consistent. I'd aim for about 16 hours a week, but it often got broken up into smaller chunks whenever I could grab time. I mostly relied on reading and writing things out since that's how I learn best, so I'd print things when I could afford to or just copy notes by hand.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on deadlines. I'd have good intentions to study regularly, but then anxiety would creep in and I'd end up procrastinating, then cramming closer to due dates. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd be stressed out trying to catch up. The unpredictability of my home situation with limited resources made it hard to stick to a fixed schedule.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I remembered an assignment was coming up or when I got a notification about something. Sometimes boredom would actually push me to check in, just to see if there was something I should be doing. I didn't log in super frequently though - maybe twice a week on average. It was more reactive than proactive, if I'm being honest.
Q4: Not really a solid routine, but I did start checking the discussion boards somewhat regularly because I found interacting with other students helped break up the monotony. After a few weeks, posting in discussions became something I'd do when I logged in, almost automatically. The CBT sessions I had actually helped me recognize some patterns in my avoidance, which made me slightly more intentional about at least opening the platform.
Q5: The video lectures were really useful - I watched a lot of them, probably over two and a half hours worth. Being able to pause and rewatch helped since I could take notes at my own pace. The discussion forums were also valuable because seeing other students' perspectives sometimes clarified things I was confused about. Reading materials I could download were great because I could access them offline later.
Q6: I often skipped or delayed the optional readings and supplementary materials. With my anxiety, I'd look at the amount of content and feel overwhelmed, so I'd just focus on what was absolutely required. Some of the interactive activities felt tedious when I was already bored with the material, so I'd skim through those quickly without really engaging. If something wasn't clearly tied to my grade, it usually got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to passing the course or if it actually helped me understand something I was struggling with. When I could tell something would show up on an exam or in an assignment, I'd put more effort in. Also, if the activity wasn't too long and I could complete it in one sitting, that helped because my motivation is pretty low and I lose steam quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford mentally. Required ones came with this pressure that cut through my boredom and low motivation - I had to do them or fail. Optional stuff just sat there making me feel guilty that I wasn't doing more, but the anxiety about required work always won out. It's like required activities had built-in urgency that optional ones lacked.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download PDFs and readings whenever possible because my internet access isn't always reliable and I couldn't afford to stream videos repeatedly. I'd print things out at the library when I had money for it, or just handwrite notes from the screen. A lot of my actual studying happened with physical notes spread out on my bed, nowhere near the LMS.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading printed materials, and none of that would show up anywhere in the system. I'd also sometimes study with a classmate in person, quizzing each other from our notes. The LMS would just show I hadn't logged in for days, but I was actually reviewing content pretty consistently offline during exam prep.
Q11: Yeah, that was basically my strategy for most readings. I'd access them once, download or copy what I needed, and then work with those materials offline for days or even weeks. It saved data and meant I could study whenever, even when the wifi was down. The system probably thought I only looked at things briefly when really I was going back to them repeatedly.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when assignments piled up and my anxiety spiked. I'd feel this heavy boredom mixed with dread - like I knew I should be doing more but couldn't make myself care enough to start. The CBT sessions helped me recognize these patterns, but in the moment it was still hard to push through. Financial stress at home didn't help either.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from worrying about money or dealing with stuff at home, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to look at. Confusion about assignments would make me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face what I didn't understand. I'd tell myself I'd figure it out later, then later would become the night before it was due.
Q14: Oh, for sure. There were times I'd have a video playing but I'd be completely zoned out, thinking about other stuff or just staring blankly. I'd scroll through readings without actually processing the words. The system would register that I watched or accessed something, but my brain wasn't really there. Boredom made it hard to stay mentally present.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, even when I knew it would stress me out later. The anxiety made starting feel impossible sometimes - like this wall I couldn't climb over. I'd tell myself I worked better under pressure, but really I was just avoiding the discomfort of engaging with material I found boring. My low resilience meant small setbacks would derail me for days.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd see a deadline approaching and finally force myself to log in and submit something. Most of my LMS activity probably clustered around due dates. Without deadlines, I'd drift and let things slide. They created the external pressure my low motivation couldn't generate internally.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect. Good feedback would give me a brief boost, but it faded quickly. Critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid that assignment type for a while. I tried to use feedback to improve, but with my low resilience, negative comments hit harder than positive ones helped. Sometimes I wouldn't check feedback for days because I was scared of what it might say.
Q18: The course structure was okay but having 16 online courses meant everything blurred together sometimes. When modules were clearly laid out with explicit deadlines, I could manage better. But when things were vague or self-paced, I'd fall behind because I lack the internal motivation to push myself. The structure needed to be tight for someone like me to stay on track.
Q19: Clear, chunked content made engagement easier - I could tackle small pieces without feeling overwhelmed. What made it harder was when there were too many options or when important information was buried in long documents. My read-write learning style meant I needed text-based materials, and when courses relied heavily on videos without transcripts, that was frustrating. The discussion requirements actually helped because they forced regular engagement.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week - logging in, clicking around, maybe watching videos but not actually completing anything because the motivation just isn't there. Or maybe they're confused about what's required and spending time trying to figure things out without actually producing work. High anxiety can make you circle around tasks without landing on them.
Q21: That sounds familiar actually. I completed 65% of assignments and passed with decent exam scores, but my login frequency was pretty low. I did a lot of work offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. Someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't trying, but I was just studying in ways that didn't register in the system. The LMS doesn't see everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, which might indicate deadline-driven behavior or consistent engagement. But they miss the quality of attention, offline studying, and emotional state. They can't tell if someone watched a video while actually learning or just had it playing in the background. They definitely can't capture anxiety or boredom affecting engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to pass. It's when you're mentally present and curious, asking questions and making connections. For me, genuine engagement is rare because of my low motivation and the boredom I feel with a lot of content. I'd say I'm more compliant than truly engaged most of the time - doing what's required without much investment.
Q24: The data wouldn't show my anxiety levels or how much mental energy it took just to log in some days. It wouldn't capture the offline studying I did or the peer discussions that happened outside the platform. It also can't show that my low engagement wasn't about not caring - it was about struggling with motivation and resilience while dealing with limited resources at home. The numbers tell a story, but it's not the whole story.
